title: High-spatiotemporal-resolution mapping of global urban change from 1985 to 2015
data_sources: Landsat archive (5/7/8) via Google Earth Engine
ESA Climate Change Initiative (ESA-CCI) land cover
Gridded Population of the World v4 (GPW)
core_conclusion: Global urban land expanded by 9,687 km² per year from 1985 to 2015 at 30 m annual resolution — four times faster than prior estimates — and at a rate substantially exceeding population growth (80% vs. 52%), meaning much new development served commercial/industrial rather than housing purposes. Approximately 70% of urban expansion occurred at the expense of agricultural land.
why_it_matters: First 30 m annual globally consistent urban-change database for three decades; reveals a four-times undercount of prior urbanization estimates; decouples urban land growth from population growth; quantifies agricultural land loss threatening food security; freely available open dataset enabling derivative climate, ecology, and planning research.
why_nature: Global scope and planetary sustainability significance; platform-level methodological step-change from coarse/episodic to 30m/annual mapping; cross-domain relevance (carbon, heat, biodiversity, food, floods); open data ethos aligned with Nature's reproducibility values.
added_value_to_my_research: GAUD annual 30m rasters as substrate for dynamic 2SFCA/R2SFCA accessibility gap detection at urban expansion fronts
GAUD validated labels as fine-tuning corpus for geospatial foundation model urban-change heads
Green-recovery signal linked to LST and health outcomes via MGWR for healthy-cities evidence chain
GAUD growth-trajectory typology as outcome variable for SHAP-interpretable sprawl-driver XGBoost model
Urban expansion fronts as spatial-constraint layer in efficiency-vs-equity facility-location optimization

related.title: Global impacts of future urban expansion on terrestrial vertebrate diversity

related.title: Trees halve urban heat island effect globally but unequal benefits only modestly mitigate climate-change warming
related.relation: connects urban vegetation (green recovery) to heat island mitigation — extends GAUD's green-recovery signal

figures.caption: Global urban area dynamics 1985–2015 at global and continental scales — documents 9,687 km² yr⁻¹ expansion rate with 95% confidence intervals
Normalized urban expansion trends at global, continental, and city-cluster scales — contrasts accelerating (Shanghai) vs. decelerating (Chicago) urbanization trajectories
Urban expansion and green recovery maps for China, USA, and India — China shows largest green recovery globally
Dominant land-cover conversion sources of new urban land per cluster (1992–2015) — approximately 70% from agricultural land
